_work.title: The Popular Grimoire Tradition, 17th–19th c.
_work.creator: various
_work.blurb: The popular grimoire tradition — the *Grimorium Verum* (first printed 1817, tradition 17th-c.), *Grand Grimoire* or *Red Dragon*, the *Petit Albert* and *Grand Albert*, the *Sixth and Seventh Books of Moses*, and the *Black Pullet* — represents the commercialised street-level end of learned ceremonial magic. These books travelled across European chapbook culture, into African-American conjure (hoodoo), German-American *Braucherei* (pow-wow), and beyond, translated into the practical magic of specific communities. Their illustrations — spirit seals, letter squares, diagrammatic talismans, scenes of devil pacts — are among the most visually striking popular-press images of any century.

entries.title: Clauneck, Spirit of the *Grimorium Verum* (Dictionnaire Infernal plate)
entries.creator: after Collin de Plancy, *Dictionnaire Infernal* (1863 ed.)
entries.summary:
Clauneck, the demon of money, riches, and financial gain, as depicted in Collin de Plancy’s *Dictionnaire Infernal* (final edition, 1863) — the most widely-circulated demonic encyclopaedia of the 19th century.

## Iconography
A horned, goat-legged figure in courtly dress, rendered in the dense cross-hatched engraving style characteristic of Louis Breton’s illustrations for de Plancy. The image synthesises the *Grimorium Verum*’s description of Clauneck as a spirit who bestows wealth and discovers hidden treasures with the popular iconography of the well-dressed devil.

## Significance
Clauneck is one of the principal spirits of the *Grimorium Verum*, the most practical and operationally-focused of the 18th-century popular grimoires. The text exists in two recensions: an Italian tradition traceable to the early 17th century and a French printed edition of 1817 falsely attributed to a 1517 Toulouse printing. Clauneck’s office — bringing money from afar, causing the discovery of treasure — reflects the grimoire’s character as a book for working magicians seeking material results. The *Dictionnaire Infernal* plate transformed the cryptic seal of a manuscript tradition into a recognisable popular image, widely reproduced through the 19th century and into the modern occult revival.

## For artists & game designers
A ready figure for a demon of commerce, a patron of merchants and thieves, or a spirit bound to bring wealth at a price. The formal attire signals an important 19th-century trope: the devil as gentleman of business.

citations.text: Jacques Collin de Plancy, *Dictionnaire Infernal* (Paris: Plon, 1863), p. 172 (plate by Louis Breton).
Joseph H. Peterson (ed.), *Grimorium Verum* (Scotts Valley: CreateSpace, 2007).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Frontispiece of the *Grimoire du Pape Honorius* (c. 1760)
entries.creator: Anonymous
entries.summary:
The frontispiece of the *Grimoire du Pape Honorius*, the audacious 18th-century French grimoire that claimed papal authority for its diabolical operations.

## Iconography
A title page or frontispiece in the crude woodcut or cheap intaglio style typical of provincial French chapbook printing: the attribution to Pope Honorius framed by decorative borders. The very existence of such imagery — the pope as sorcerer — signals the anti-clerical current running through the popular grimoire tradition.

## Significance
The *Grimoire du Pape Honorius* (first attested in print c. 1760, alleged date of 1629 false) is a product of the French popular grimoire market, exploiting the legendary authority of Pope Honorius III to whom the earlier medieval *Sworn Book of Honorius* was also attributed. It circulated across France and into the French Caribbean, becoming an important text in Haitian and Louisiana Voodoo traditions, where the figure of Papa Legba partially mapped onto Honorius. Unlike the learned *Sworn Book*, this text is short, practical, and explicitly diabolical: it instructs the user to summon Lucifer, Beelzebub, and Astaroth with animal sacrifices and specific prayers.

## For artists & game designers
The grimoire-as-forbidden-papal-document is a powerful prop: the suggestion that even the Pope maintained a black book of diabolical operations challenged both ecclesiastical authority and the boundary between orthodox prayer and operative magic.

citations.text: Arthur Edward Waite, *The Book of Black Magic and of Pacts* (London: Redway, 1898), pp. 77–79 (on the Grimoire of Pope Honorius).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Lucifuge Rofocale, Prime Minister of Hell (*Grand Grimoire*)
entries.creator: Anonymous
entries.summary:
Lucifuge Rofocale, the demon designated Prime Minister of Hell in the *Grand Grimoire* (also titled *Le Dragon Rouge*, the Red Dragon), the most operationally explicit of all popular grimoires.

## Iconography
Lucifuge rendered as a classical devil figure: horned, goat-legged, bat-winged. The image participates in the standardised popular iconography of the 19th-century infernal encyclopaedia while illustrating a specific character unique to the *Grand Grimoire*’s elaborate hierarchy of Hell.

## Significance
The *Grand Grimoire* (first printed c. 1750–1800, falsely dated to 1522) is centred on a pact with Lucifuge Rofocale, a demon invented or preserved in this text alone. The name likely derives from *luce fugere* (to flee the light), making him a dark counterpart to Lucifer. The grimoire’s pact-working is extraordinarily explicit: it specifies the exact words to say, the animal sacrifices to perform, and the document the magician signs in blood. This is ceremonial magic stripped to its most commercial, transactional form. The *Grand Grimoire* circulated throughout France, the French Caribbean, and New Orleans, feeding into Voodoo and Hoodoo practice, where its pact structure influenced the figure of the crossroads deal.

## For artists & game designers
Lucifuge is ideal for devil-pact mechanics: a bureaucrat of Hell who actually wants to be left alone and only appears when properly compelled. The pact document in the *Grand Grimoire* is one of the most dramatically realised in any magical text.

citations.text: A. E. Waite, *The Book of Black Magic and of Pacts* (1898), ch. 5.
Darcy Küntz (trans.), *The Grand Grimoire* (Sequim: Holmes, 2008).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Frontispiece of the *Sixth and Seventh Books of Moses* (1880 ed.)
entries.creator: Anonymous
key_concepts: Sixth and Seventh Books of Moses
entries.summary:
The frontispiece of the 1880 Stuttgart edition of the *Sechstes und Siebentes Buch Mosis* (Sixth and Seventh Books of Moses), the most widely distributed folk magic book of the 19th and early 20th centuries.

## Iconography
A title page with Moses as the commanding figure: the prophet as master magician, presented in the tradition of Mosaic theurgy that runs from the Talmudic wonder-tales through the medieval Jewish magical tradition.

## Significance
The *Sixth and Seventh Books of Moses* was first published in Stuttgart in 1849, claiming to be the lost continuation of the Pentateuch containing Moses’s magical secrets. It synthesised German-Jewish Kabbalistic material, solomonic seals, and German folk-magic traditions into a single volume that was both a vernacular grimoire and a cultural object. Its impact was enormous: German immigrants carried it to Pennsylvania, where it fed into *Braucherei* (pow-wow) practice; African-American workers encountered it in the Jim Crow South, where it became a canonical hoodoo text, ranking alongside the *Grand Grimoire* in conjure shops. Carl Taber’s English translation of 1880 drove its American diffusion. The seals, diagrams, and magical squares in its pages became standard elements of African-American folk magic practice.

## For artists & game designers
The book’s authority rested on its Old Testament framing: Moses as the original magician, his powers available to ordinary people through correct use of the seals. A prop with cross-cultural resonance in African-American, German-American, and Jewish-American communities.

citations.text: Maureen Korp (ed.), *The Sacred Geography of the American Mound Builders* (1990).
Carolyn Morrow Long, *Spiritual Merchants* (Knoxville: University of Tennessee Press, 2001), ch. 2 (on the Sixth and Seventh Books in African-American conjure).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Seal Figure 104 from the *Sixth and Seventh Books of Moses* (1880 ed.)
entries.creator: Anonymous
key_concepts: Sixth and Seventh Books of Moses
entries.summary:
One of the talismanic seal figures from the 1880 edition of the *Sixth and Seventh Books of Moses*, representative of the book’s dense visual programme of magical diagrams.

## Iconography
A circular or geometrically-structured magical seal combining divine or angelic names (often in Hebrew or pseudo-Hebrew), numerical squares, and diagrammatic figures. These seals were understood by users as instruments of power — to be drawn, worn, or placed — rather than merely illustrations.

## Significance
The seals of the *Sixth and Seventh Books* constitute one of the largest systematic collections of talismanic imagery in popular print culture. They draw on the Solomonic seal tradition (via the *Key of Solomon* and *Sixth Book*’s own internal Kabbalistic claims) but the actual source of many seals is obscure; scholars have identified German Pietist and Rosicrucian material, as well as genuine adaptations of Kabbalistic letter-magic. In the hoodoo tradition, individual seals were clipped from the book and used as physical talismans — folded into mojo bags, placed under mattresses, tucked into wallets. This material use of a printed page as a functional magical object is one of the distinctive features of the African-American reception of the text.

## For artists & game designers
Each seal is a distinct artefact: a small powerful diagram in the tradition of the planetary seal and angelic character. An ideal source for game currency, collectible items, or magical tokens with distinct visual identities.

citations.text: *Sixth and Seventh Books of Moses* (Stuttgart & New York: Scheible, Rieger & Sattler, 1849; English trans. Carl Taber, 1880).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Frontispiece Portrait from *Le Grand Albert* (the *Secrets of Albertus Magnus*)
entries.creator: Anonymous (French popular press)
entries.summary:
Frontispiece portrait from one of the many French popular editions of *Le Grand Albert* — the enormously influential compendium of natural secrets attributed to Albertus Magnus (c. 1200–80), one of the most prolific publishers’ fictions of the early modern French book trade.

## Iconography
A portrait of the scholar-magician Albertus Magnus in Dominican robes, presented as authority and guarantor of the text’s secrets. The image conforms to the conventions of early printed book frontispieces: the learned man as source of arcane knowledge.

## Significance
The *Grand Albert* (*Les admirables secrets d’Albert le Grand*, first French chapbook edition Lyon c. 1740) is a compilation of natural magic, medical recipes, erotic talismans, and astrological correspondences presented under the name of the medieval scholastic Albertus Magnus. It was issued in cheap duodecimo format at Lyon, Paris, and dozens of provincial presses throughout the 18th and 19th centuries and became one of the most widely distributed books in France. Its companion, the *Petit Albert* (*Secrets merveilleux de la magie naturelle et cabalistique du Petit Albert*, c. 1722), covered similar ground at greater length with more explicitly magical content. Together they are the backbone of the French folk-magic tradition: carried by itinerant workers, shared among peasants, smuggled across the Atlantic into the French Caribbean. Scholars of French popular culture (particularly Robert Muchembled and Suzanne Sulzberger) have traced their social function in early modern communities.

## For artists & game designers
The scholar-magician as brand: Albertus Magnus’s name sold folk-medicine, love-charms, and treasure-finding recipes to generations of European peasants. A perfect figure for a wizard’s almanac prop or a wandering merchant of magical secrets.

citations.text: Robert Muchembled, *Popular Culture and Elite Culture in France, 1400–1750* (Baton Rouge: LSU Press, 1985).
Owen Davies, *Popular Magic: Cunning-folk in English History* (London: Hambledon Continuum, 2007), ch. 2.
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Magic Circle of the Elements from the *Heptameron* (Agrippa’s *Fourth Book*)
entries.creator: after Pietro d’Abano (attrib.) / Henry Cornelius Agrippa (Fourth Book, attrib.)
entries.date: 16th–17th c. tradition; this image after early printed editions
entries.summary:
The conjuration circle of the four elements from the *Heptameron, or Magical Elements* attributed to Pietro d’Abano, as it appears appended to the apocryphal *Fourth Book of Occult Philosophy* attributed to Agrippa.

## Iconography
A diagrammatic ritual circle incorporating the names of the four elements, their associated angels and spirits, directional attributions, and protective divine names. Concentric rings of inscription surround a working space: the visual grammar of the ceremonial magic circle reduced to its most schematic printed form.

## Significance
The *Heptameron* (first printed 1496, attributed to the Paduan physician Pietro d’Abano, though likely of later composition) is a compact manual for the conjuration of spirits via the seven days of the week and their associated angels. It was appended to numerous editions of Agrippa’s *De Occulta Philosophia* as a spurious *Fourth Book* and thus travelled very widely in the 16th–18th centuries: a Renaissance text that became a popular grimoire by transmission. Its circle design is the direct ancestor of hundreds of later conjuration circles in manuscript and print grimoires across Europe, and its day-and-hour angelic system underlies the *Ars Paulina* and sections of the Lemegeton.

## For artists & game designers
The Heptameron circle is the archetype of the grimoire conjuration diagram: a precisely-inscribed working space that physically separates the magician from the spirit being summoned. Directly usable as a game mechanic diagram or floor design.

citations.text: Robert Turner (trans.), *Heptameron, or Magical Elements* (London, 1655), repr. in *Fourth Book of Occult Philosophy* (Lake Worth: Ibis Press, 2006).
Frank Klaassen, *The Transformations of Magic* (Philadelphia: Penn State Press, 2013), ch. 3.
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Title Page of the *Arbatel of Magic* (*Arbatel de Magia Veterum*, 1575)
entries.creator: Anonymous
figures: Olympic spirits: Aratron, Bethor, Phaleg, Och, Hagith, Ophiel, Phul
entries.summary:
The title page of the *Arbatel de Magia Veterum* (Arbatel of Ancient Magic), first published at Basel in 1575, one of the most theologically sophisticated and unusual grimoires of the Renaissance.

## Iconography
A Renaissance title page in the standard Basel printing style: title, attribution, printer’s device, place and date. The visual simplicity belies the text’s extraordinary claims: that a complete magical system could be expressed in 49 aphorisms and that its seven Olympic spirits each govern a cosmic sphere.

## Significance
The *Arbatel* is unique among Renaissance grimoires in several ways: it is brief (one of the nine projected books survives), it presents magic in aphoristic rather than procedural form, and its tone is explicitly Christian and ethical. The seven Olympic spirits — Aratron (Saturn), Bethor (Jupiter), Phaleg (Mars), Och (Sun), Hagith (Venus), Ophiel (Mercury), Phul (Moon) — are not Solomonic demons but planetary governors presented almost as angels. The work was enormously influential on later occultism: the Olympic spirits resurface in the works of Paracelsus, in Renaissance natural magic, and in modern Thelemic and Chaos Magic traditions. Its insistence on the magician’s moral virtue as prerequisite for success distinguishes it from the instrumental, deal-making tone of the popular grimoires proper.

## For artists & game designers
Seven Olympic spirits with planetary offices, each governable for limited periods, each requiring virtue rather than compulsion: an ideal non-demonic spirit system for morally complex world-building.

citations.text: *Arbatel of Magic* (Basel, 1575; repr. Joseph Peterson ed., Esoteric Archives, online).
Christopher Lehrich, *The Language of Demons and Angels* (Leiden: Brill, 2003), ch. 5.
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Faust and Mephistopheles at Chess (19th-century popular print)
entries.creator: Anonymous (German popular press, 19th c.)
entries.summary:
Faust and Mephistopheles engaged in a chess game: a popular 19th-century image of the devil’s compact as a battle of wits, drawing on both the Goethe’s literary *Faust* and the older *Faustbuch* chapbook tradition.

## Iconography
The scholar-magician and his demonic familiar face each other across a chessboard, the game functioning as a visual metaphor for the devil pact: a contest of souls in which the outcome is predetermined by the terms of the contract, yet whose drama rests on the possibility of escape.

## Significance
The historical Johann Georg Faust (fl. c. 1480–1540) was an itinerant German necromancer and charlatan documented in the letters of Philipp Melanchthon and others. His legend crystallised in the *Historia von D. Johann Fausten* (Frankfurt, 1587), the chapbook (*Volksbuch*) that initiated one of the most generative literary traditions in European culture. The Faust story is also the gateway through which the devil-pact grimoire reached German popular consciousness: the *Faustbuch* was read as both moral tale and practical manual, and pseudo-Faustian magic books (*Doktor Faustens Hollenzwang*, *Schwarzes Rabenbuch*) circulated as genuine grimoires in German-speaking communities throughout the 17th–19th centuries. The chess variant of the pact story appears in folk legend (the “Faust of the crossroads” motif) and in 19th-century popular prints.

## For artists & game designers
The chess-with-the-devil image is an enduring archetype: the pact as a game played on the devil’s own board, where the soul’s fate hinges on an apparently fair contest that is actually rigged from the start.

citations.text: *Historia von D. Johann Fausten* (Frankfurt: Spies, 1587); trans. P. F. as *The History of the Damnable Life and Deserved Death of Doctor John Faustus* (London, 1592).
E. M. Butler, *The Myth of the Magus* (Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 1948), chs. 10–11.
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Latin *Picatrix*, Kraków MS 793, fol. 189r (15th c.)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Latin translation, 13th c.; this MS 15th c.)
entries.date: 15th c. MS (Latin trans. 13th c.; Arabic original c. 11th c.)
entries.summary:
A page from the Latin *Picatrix* (the *Ghayat al-Hakim*’s Latin translation), Kraków, Biblioteka Jagiellońska, MS 793, fol. 189r, a 15th-century Polish copy of one of the most influential magical textbooks ever written.

## Iconography
A page of Latin text with diagrams or tables: the characteristic layout of a working manual combining theoretical exposition (on the astral basis of magic) with practical tables of correspondences, talismanic forms, and instructions for planetary operations.

## Significance
The *Ghayat al-Hakim* (Goal of the Sage, c. 11th c. CE, attributed to Maslama al-Qurtubi) was translated into Latin as *Picatrix* in the court of Alfonso X of Castile (c. 1256) and became the foundational text of learned European talismanic magic for the next four centuries. It is a systematic treatise on astral magic: the construction of talismans that capture and channel planetary influences, the use of ritual timing, incense, suffumigation, and image-making. Its scope — four books covering cosmology, planetary spirits, talisman theory, and worked examples — made it the reference text for every subsequent magician who wanted to work with planetary forces. Cornelius Agrippa, Marsilio Ficino, and John Dee all knew it. The Kraków manuscript is a rare Polish witness to the wide transmission of the text.

## For artists & game designers
A systematic planetary-influence system with specific talismans for each planet’s favourable and unfavourable faces: the *Picatrix* provides the cosmological engine for an astrological magic mechanic.

citations.text: Greer & Warnock (trans.), *Picatrix: A Medieval Treatise on Astral Magic* (State College: Adocentyn Press, 2011).
David Pingree, ‘Some of the Sources of the *Ghayat al-Hakim*’, *Journal of the Warburg and Courtauld Institutes* 43 (1980), 1–15.
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Arabic *Picatrix* (*Ghayat al-Hakim*), Hamidiye Library MS 852, fol. 45v
entries.creator: Anonymous (attributed to Maslama al-Qurtubi, c. 11th c.)
entries.date: MS date uncertain (original c. 11th c. CE)
entries.place: Islamic world (this MS: Ottoman Empire / Istanbul)
entries.summary:
A page from the Arabic *Ghayat al-Hakim* (Goal of the Sage), known in the West as the *Picatrix*, from the Hamidiye manuscript collection of the Süleymaniye Library, Istanbul.

## Iconography
Arabic text in a clear hand, possibly with diagrams, tables, or talismanic figures: the characteristic visual form of the Islamic occult treatise, operating at the intersection of philosophical cosmology and operative magic.

## Significance
In its Arabic original the *Ghayat al-Hakim* is a 400-year older text than the Latin *Picatrix* and represents a distinct tradition: it synthesises Neoplatonic cosmology, Hermetic philosophy, Sabian star-worship, and practical talisman-making into a comprehensive magical cosmology. Its theory of astral correspondences — the idea that talismans capture planetary influences by combining the right materials, timing, and imagery — is derived from Plotinus and Proclus but adapted for operative Arabic practice. The Hamidiye manuscript is an Ottoman-period copy preserving the Arabic tradition that the Latin translation (important as it was for European magic) only partially represents. Reading the two manuscript traditions against each other — as scholars like David Pingree and Liana De Girolami Cheney have done — reveals significant differences in emphasis and content.

## For artists & game designers
The Arabic *Picatrix* presents astral magic as a science: a rigorous system of timing, materials, and image-making. Ideal for a magic system that rewards precision and cosmological knowledge over brute supernatural force.

citations.text: Hellmut Ritter & Martin Plessner (eds. & trans.), *Picatrix: Das Ziel des Weisen von Pseudo-Magriti* (London: Warburg Institute, 1962).
Liana De Girolami Cheney & John Hendrix (eds.), *Neoplatonism and the Arts* (2016), ch. on Picatrix.
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Sigil Page from *Sefer Raziel ha-Malakh* (1701 printed edition)
entries.creator: Anonymous (compiled medieval period; this ed. Amsterdam 1701)
entries.summary:
A page of angelic sigils from the 1701 Amsterdam printed edition of *Sefer Raziel ha-Malakh* (Book of Raziel the Angel), the most important Jewish magical encyclopaedia of the medieval period.

## Iconography
A dense page of abstract angelic signatures or characters: non-figural glyphs combining geometric forms, letter-derived strokes, and abstract marks. These sigils are the visual vocabulary of Jewish angel magic — the unique signatures by which angels are identified, invoked, and bound, analogous in function to the seals of the Solomonic demon tradition but oriented toward angelic rather than demonic operation.

## Significance
*Sefer Raziel* claims to transmit the magical wisdom given by the angel Raziel to Adam after the expulsion from Eden, preserved in a book written on sapphire. In reality it is a medieval Hebrew compilation (likely 13th c., probably composed in the Rhineland or Provence) incorporating older material from the *Hekhalot* literature, astral magic, incantation texts, and angelology. Its Amsterdam printed edition (1701) made it available to a much wider audience and cemented its status as a community talisman — copies were placed in homes as protective objects, used as amulets against fire. The book’s influence on Christian Kabbalah (via Johannes Reuchlin and the broader Hebraica movement) and on the learned European occult tradition is substantial.

## For artists & game designers
Angelic sigils as a non-demonic system of magical signatures: each angel has a unique glyph, invoking it correctly requires knowing the right character, and the characters can be inscribed on amulets for protective purposes.

citations.text: *Sefer Raziel ha-Malakh* (Amsterdam, 1701; repr. New York: KTAV, 2000).
Gideon Bohak, *Ancient Jewish Magic* (Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2008), ch. 9.
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Title Page, *The Book of the Sacred Magic of Abramelin the Mage* (1898 trans.)
entries.creator: S. L. MacGregor Mathers (trans. & ed.); original Abraham of Worms (15th c.)
entries.place: London, England (ed.); original: Germany (15th c. MS)
entries.summary:
The title page of S. L. MacGregor Mathers’ 1898 English translation of *The Book of the Sacred Magic of Abramelin the Mage* — one of the most influential grimoire editions of the modern occult revival.

## Iconography
A Victorian title page in the ornate typographic style of Watkins occult publishing: title, author, translator, and publisher information framed by decorative rules. The austerity of the design belies the book’s contents — a 15th-century German Jewish manuscript presenting an 18-month magical operation to contact the Holy Guardian Angel.

## Significance
*The Book of the Sacred Magic of Abramelin the Mage* is a 15th-century manuscript (Bibliothèque de l’Arsenal, Paris MS 2351; German originals: Dresden, Wolfenbüttel) attributed to Abraham of Worms, a Jewish merchant who claims to have received the system from an Egyptian sage named Abramelin. Its central operation — the 18-month retreat, prayer, and self-purification to achieve Knowledge and Conversation with the Holy Guardian Angel — became the definitive magical objective of the Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn and, through Aleister Crowley’s *Liber ABA* and his own attempted Abramelin working (Boleskine, 1899–1900), the central axis of 20th-century ceremonial magic. The book’s word-square talismans, used in the operation’s final phase to bind demons to the magician’s service, are among the most distinctive visual elements of any grimoire.

## For artists & game designers
The Holy Guardian Angel concept — a personal divine protector whose contact transforms the magician — is one of the most game-able elements of the Western magical tradition: a quest structure (18 months of preparation) with a specific reward (cosmic protection, demonic servitude).

citations.text: S. L. MacGregor Mathers (trans.), *The Book of the Sacred Magic of Abramelin the Mage* (London: Watkins, 1898).
Georg Dehn & Steven Guth (eds.), *The Book of Abramelin: A New Translation* (Lake Worth: Ibis Press, 2006).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Baal, King of Hell (after Collin de Plancy, *Dictionnaire Infernal*, 1863)
entries.creator: Louis Breton (engraver) after Collin de Plancy, *Dictionnaire Infernal* (1863)
entries.summary:
Baal (the Ars Goetia’s first spirit, Bael) as depicted by Louis Breton in the 1863 edition of Collin de Plancy’s *Dictionnaire Infernal*, the most widely-reproduced image of this demon in the Western tradition.

## Iconography
Bael rendered as a composite figure with three heads — a cat, a toad, and a human — above a spider’s body with human legs, representing the traditional description in the Ars Goetia: the King appears with these three heads and speaks with a hoarse voice. Louis Breton’s engraving for de Plancy fixed this visual type for all subsequent depictions.

## Significance
Bael (from the ancient Near Eastern Ba‘al, “lord”) is the first and highest-ranking king of the Ars Goetia in the *Lemegeton* — the *Lesser Key of Solomon* — commanding 66 legions of spirits and able to make the conjurer invisible. The Breton engraving transformed the abstract textual description of a spirit hierarchy into a visual gallery of demonic figures that shaped popular understanding of goetic spirits for 150 years. *Dictionnaire Infernal*’s plates were reproduced in newspapers, chapbooks, and popular encyclopaedias across Europe and North America, making them the primary visual source for modern Western demonology.

## For artists & game designers
Bael’s three-headed composite form is one of the most visually distinctive demons in the grimoire tradition: a figure that combines royal authority (the human face, the hoarse commanding voice) with bestial ugliness. The first demon in the Ars Goetia and a natural choice for a supreme demonic antagonist.

citations.text: Jacques Collin de Plancy, *Dictionnaire Infernal* (Paris: Plon, 1863), plate by Louis Breton.
S. L. MacGregor Mathers & Aleister Crowley (eds.), *The Goetia: The Lesser Key of Solomon the King* (1904), entry 1: Bael.
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Page from *The Grand Grimoire* (*Red Dragon*) in Thomas Frognall Dibdin’s Tour (1821)
entries.creator: Thomas Frognall Dibdin (illustrating bibliographer)
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons / Flickr.
entries.summary:
An image from Thomas Frognall Dibdin’s *Bibliographical, Antiquarian and Picturesque Tour in France and Germany* (1821), in which the celebrated bibliophile documents his encounter with the *Grand Grimoire* in a French collection — one of the earliest scholarly references to the text in the anglophone world.

## Iconography
An engraved plate in Dibdin’s characteristic picturesque-tour style: antiquarian atmosphere, scholarly documentation of forbidden texts, the physical book as object of fascination and mild horror.

## Significance
Dibdin’s description of encountering the *Grand Grimoire* in a Paris collection is significant for the history of grimoire scholarship: it marks the moment when the popular magical text becomes an object of educated curiosity rather than clerical prosecution. The image records the early 19th century’s shift from grimoire as functional magical tool to grimoire as bibliographic curiosity — a transition that prefigures the full occult revival of the later 19th century. Owen Davies notes in *Grimoires* (2009) that the bibliophile tradition of collecting forbidden books was itself a form of occult prestige.

## For artists & game designers
The scholarly encounter with the forbidden text is a perennial narrative: the antiquarian who buys a grimoire at auction, the librarian who discovers a magical text in a locked case. This image documents that encounter in historical reality.

citations.text: Thomas Frognall Dibdin, *A Bibliographical, Antiquarian and Picturesque Tour in France and Germany*, 2 vols. (London: Payne & Foss, 1821).
Owen Davies, *Grimoires: A History of Magic Books* (Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2009), ch. 5.
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Devil’s Pact: Faust and Mephistopheles (Julius Nisle, c. 1840)
entries.creator: Julius Nisle (German illustrator, fl. 1830s–40s)
entries.summary:
Julius Nisle’s mid-19th-century illustration of the Faust pact: the scholar-magician signing the compact with Mephistopheles in blood, the central image of the devil-pact grimoire tradition.

## Iconography
Faust at a desk or lectern, quill in hand, signing the demonic contract in blood while Mephistopheles supervises or offers the document. The image belongs to the mature Romantic visual tradition of the Faust story: detailed period costume, dramatic shadow, the contrast between the learned scholar’s study and the supernatural intruder.

## Significance
The blood pact is the most legally-specific element of the popular grimoire tradition: the *Grand Grimoire*, the *Grimorium Verum*, and the Faust chapbooks all describe a written contract signed in blood as the mechanism of the devil compact. Nisle’s illustration dates from the period when the Faust story was being transformed by Goethe’s *Faust Part II* (1832) from chapbook entertainment into high-literary myth, while simultaneously being re-popularised in cheap illustrated editions for mass audiences. The blood pact image circulated widely in both registers — elite theatre programs and penny chapbooks alike — making it the most culturally available image of the devil’s compact in European popular culture.

## For artists & game designers
The signed demonic contract is a game mechanic as much as an image: it specifies terms, sets duration, and creates a binding obligation. The visual grammar — formal document, blood pen, demonic witness — translates directly into rule-sets for pact-magic systems.

citations.text: Julius Nisle, illustrations for Goethe, *Faust* (Stuttgart, c. 1840).
Jan Wierda, ‘Visual Representations of the Faust Pact in the 19th Century’, *Faust Studies* (2015).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Title Page of Reginald Scot’s *The Discoverie of Witchcraft* (1584)
entries.creator: Reginald Scot
entries.summary:
The title page of Reginald Scot’s *The Discoverie of Witchcraft* (London, 1584), the most important English-language sceptical treatment of witchcraft and magic of the Elizabethan period — which also became, paradoxically, one of the most significant magical sourcebooks of the 17th century.

## Iconography
An Elizabethan title page in black-letter and Roman type: Scot’s name, the full title (“Discoverie of Witchcraft, wherein the Lewd dealing of Witches and Witchmongers is Notable Detected...”), the date and printer. The visual austerity of the learned text signals its rationalist, anti-superstition intent.

## Significance
Scot’s *Discoverie* (1584) is a paradoxical text: written to debunk witchcraft by showing that its supposed effects were either fraudulent or diabolical delusion, it also provides extraordinarily detailed descriptions of conjuration rituals, necromancy, and magical operations — sourced from continental demonological literature — that made it a functional grimoire in the hands of 17th-century practitioners. King James I famously ordered copies burned on his accession (1603). The book’s Book XV, containing detailed necromantic operations, was extracted and circulated as a working manual. It is a primary source for Elizabethan stage magic (Shakespeare drew on it for *A Midsummer Night’s Dream*) and a major conduit for Continental magical material into English.

## For artists & game designers
The sceptic’s grimoire: a text that tries to debunk magic but preserves so many authentic rituals that it becomes a handbook. A fine model for a lore-dump artefact that is simultaneously scholarly and dangerous.

citations.text: Reginald Scot, *The Discoverie of Witchcraft* (London: William Brome, 1584); repr. ed. Montague Summers (New York: Dover, 1972).
Sydney Anglo, *The Damned Art: Essays in the Literature of Witchcraft* (London: Routledge, 1977), ch. 5 (on Scot).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: *La Main de Gloire* — the Hand of Glory (French popular print, 19th c.)
entries.creator: Anonymous (French popular press / broadsheet)
entries.summary:
A French popular print or broadsheet featuring *La Main de Gloire* — the Hand of Glory, one of the most dramatically charged objects in the European folk-magic tradition.

## Iconography
A broadsheet narrative or image depicting the Hand of Glory: the preserved hand of a hanged man, with candles between the fingers or the whole hand as candleholder, used to immobilise the inhabitants of a house while thieves worked inside.

## Significance
The Hand of Glory (*main de gloire*, from *mandragore* / mandrake, by popular etymology) is one of the clearest examples of the folk-magic object that exists simultaneously in learned grimoire literature and in popular practice. Its preparation — the hand of a hanged criminal, dried, used as a candle or candle-holder, rendering sleeping occupants immovable — is described in the *Petit Albert* (c. 1722) and in numerous later popular magic books. Physical examples survive: the Whitby Museum (England) holds a genuine example, found in a Yorkshire cottage wall in 1935. The object bridges the grimoire tradition and the criminal underworld: magic as a tool of burglary rather than of spiritual aspiration, grounded in the most transgressive possible material (a criminal corpse).

## For artists & game designers
The Hand of Glory is a prop with clear mechanical function (paralysis / immobility) and unmistakable visual identity (a human hand holding candles). It translates directly into game items, heist mechanics, or dark artefact design.

citations.text: *Secrets merveilleux de la magie naturelle et cabalistique du Petit Albert* (Lyon, c. 1722), on the Main de Gloire.
Owen Davies, *Popular Magic: Cunning-folk in English History* (2007), p. 89.
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Albertus Magnus — Scholastic Magus (portrait, popular edition)
entries.creator: Anonymous
entries.summary:
A popular portrait of Albertus Magnus (Albert the Great, c. 1200–80), the Dominican scholastic whose name became a franchise for popular magic books in the 18th and 19th centuries.

## Iconography
Albertus Magnus in Dominican habit, presented as a scholarly authority: the book-in-hand or pointing gesture of the learned teacher. The image functions as the authority-figure frontispiece common to many popular grimoire editions.

## Significance
Albertus Magnus was one of the great 13th-century natural philosophers, a teacher of Thomas Aquinas and a genuine pioneer of empirical natural science. He wrote on animals, minerals, plants, astronomy, and the limits of magic in his systematic *Speculum Astronomiae*. But by the 18th century his name had been appropriated for a completely different tradition: the *Secrets merveilleux d’Albertus Magnus* (*Grand Albert*) and the *Petit Albert* were cheap French popular magic books that bore no authentic relationship to his actual work. The appropriation worked because Albertus’s reputation as a natural philosopher made him a plausible author for books of natural secrets — and because the prestige of the scholastic tradition lent authority to texts that would otherwise have been prosecuted as sorcery. The same strategy operated for the *Sixth and Seventh Books of Moses* (Moses as author) and the *Grimoire du Pape Honorius* (the Pope as author).

## For artists & game designers
Albertus Magnus as the scholar-magician whose actual learning was respectable but whose name was franchised for the popular magic trade: a figure that collapses the boundary between natural philosophy and operative magic.

citations.text: Irven Resnick & Kenneth Kitchell (eds.), *Albert the Great: A Selective Annotated Bibliography* (Tempe: ACMRS, 2004).
Lynn Thorndike, *A History of Magic and Experimental Science*, vol. 2 (New York: Columbia UP, 1923), ch. on Albertus.
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Seal of Approbation from *Cyprianus*, 18th c. (Wellcome Collection)
entries.creator: Anonymous (18th c.)
entries.rights: CC BY 4.0. Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
The Seal of Approbation from an 18th-century edition of *Cyprianus*, the Scandinavian black book grimoire attributed to the martyr-magician Saint Cyprian of Antioch.

## Iconography
A formal seal or approbation mark: the visual gesture that claims clerical or royal approval for the text, mimicking the *imprimatur* of licensed printing. The seal functions as both authenticity claim and protective talisman.

## Significance
*Cyprianus* (also called *Svarteboken*, the Black Book) is the Scandinavian and Iberian popular grimoire tradition centred on Saint Cyprian of Antioch, a pagan sorcerer who converted to Christianity after failing to seduce the virgin Justina and was subsequently martyred (d. 304 CE). His legend made him simultaneously a great sinner, a great magician, and a great saint — the ideal patron for a tradition of Christian folk magic that operates through prayer, saints’ invocations, and demonic compulsion in the same breath. The Scandinavian *Cyprianus* tradition (documented from the 17th century) circulated in handwritten black books (*svartebøker*) and printed chapbooks across Norway, Sweden, and Denmark; the Iberian tradition (“El Ciprianillo”) did the same across Portugal, Spain, and Brazil, where Saint Cyprian remains a patron of folk magicians, macumba practitioners, and *benzedores*.

## For artists & game designers
Saint Cyprian is a unique archetype: the sorcerer-saint who retains his magical powers after conversion, whose authority comes from both demonic mastery and Christian grace. The Scandinavian black book tradition translates directly into Nordic folk-horror aesthetics.

citations.text: Alexei Kondratiev & Owen Davies, ‘Cyprianus Traditions in Northern Europe and the Iberian World’, in Davies (ed.), *The Oxford Illustrated History of Witchcraft and Magic* (Oxford: OUP, 2017).
Wellcome Collection, London, object L0036621.
Wikimedia Commons (CC BY 4.0)

entries.title: Seal of Bael, First Spirit of the Ars Goetia
entries.creator: after the Lemegeton / Ars Goetia
entries.summary:
The seal (*sigillum*) of Bael (Baal), the first and highest-ranking King of the Ars Goetia — the first book of the *Lemegeton* or Lesser Key of Solomon.

## Iconography
An abstract line-seal combining curves, crosses, and points: the graphic character by which Bael is identified, summoned, and bound. Such seals are inscribed on the conjurer’s Lamen (chest-piece) and on the Triangle of Art, compelled to appear in it.

## Significance
Bael is the first spirit listed in the Ars Goetia and commands 66 legions of inferior spirits. His three heads (cat, toad, human) can be combined or appear separately. His office is invisibility. As the first entry in the Goetia, his seal is the opening image of the most influential grimoire produced in 17th-century England. The Goetia seals — 72 abstract glyphs, each unique to its spirit, each derived from some combination of planetary characters, Hebrew letters, and traditional spirit-signatures — are among the most visually distinctive elements of the popular grimoire tradition. This seal exists here alongside the Clauneck/Grimorium Verum material to illustrate the contrast between the Goetia’s geometric abstraction and the Dictionnaire Infernal’s pictorial naturalism.

## For artists & game designers
A seal system with 72 distinct glyphs, each commanding a different number of legions and serving a specific office: the most fully-elaborated magical signature system in the Western grimoire tradition.

citations.text: S. L. MacGregor Mathers & Aleister Crowley (eds.), *The Goetia: The Lesser Key of Solomon the King* (1904), spirit 1.
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Mephistopheles Instructs Faust’s Student (Gustav Nehrlich, 19th c.)
entries.creator: Gustav Nehrlich (German illustrator, 19th c.)
entries.summary:
Gustav Nehrlich’s 19th-century illustration of Mephistopheles — impersonating Faust — receiving and instructing a student in the famous scene from Goethe’s *Faust Part I*, depicting the devil as academic authority.

## Iconography
Mephistopheles in academic robes behind a desk or lectern, engaged in the instruction of Wagner or another student: the devil as scholar, the demonic pact expressed through the authority of the academic institution rather than the magical circle. The scene captures Goethe’s satire of university learning while drawing on the popular tradition of the devil in disguise.

## Significance
The Student Scene (*Studierzimmer* section) of Goethe’s *Faust Part I* (1808) is a sustained satirical commentary on academic knowledge as a form of demonic contract: Mephistopheles advises the student to study systematically, like a Devil. Nehrlich’s illustration extends the popular-press tradition of Faust illustration into the literary-critical register, contributing to the vast visual ecosystem of Goethe’s *Faust* that functioned as a vernacular mythology in 19th-century Germany. The Faust myth bridges the gap between the popular grimoire (the *Faustbuch*, the *Höllenzwang*) and literary culture (Goethe, Marlowe) in a way unique in European magical history.

## For artists & game designers
The devil-as-academic-authority trope: a demon who operates through institutional structures rather than through explicit pact-signing. A model for demonic corruption that works through systems rather than through supernatural intervention.

citations.text: J. W. von Goethe, *Faust Part I* (Cotta, 1808), Studierzimmer scene (lines 1851–2050).
Gustav Nehrlich, illustrations for Goethe’s *Faust* (19th c.).
Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: Moina Mathers as Isis, *Abramelin* Workings (portrait, c. 1900)
entries.creator: Anonymous photographer
entries.summary:
Portrait of Moina Mathers (Mina Bergson Mathers, 1865–1928), artist and High Priestess of the Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn, in her role as Isis — likely connected to the ritual context of the *Abramelin* workings.

## Iconography
Moina Mathers in ritual costume as the goddess Isis: Egyptian-style headdress or crown, formal seated or standing pose, the attributes of the divine feminine as understood by the late Victorian occult imagination. The image belongs to the Golden Dawn practice of “being” a divine figure in ritual rather than merely invoking it.

## Significance
Moina Mathers was a central figure in the Golden Dawn: a trained artist (Slade School), she designed the ritual implements, costumes, and pictorial programmes of the Order’s ceremonies. As wife of S. L. MacGregor Mathers, who translated the *Abramelin* text in 1898, she was directly involved in the Order’s engagement with that grimoire. Aleister Crowley’s attempted Abramelin working at Boleskine House (1899–1900) — left incomplete, with dire consequences according to Crowley — made the text central to 20th-century magical culture. This portrait documents the living ritual context in which the translated grimoire was actually used: not as a collector’s curiosity but as a working manual.

## For artists & game designers
The grimoire-practitioner as divine avatar: a magician who does not merely invoke a god but becomes one in ritual. The Golden Dawn’s Egyptian ceremonialism is a visual grammar still active in fantasy and occult design.

citations.text: Mary K. Greer, *Women of the Golden Dawn* (Rochester: Park Street Press, 1995), ch. on Moina Mathers.
S. L. MacGregor Mathers (trans.), *The Book of the Sacred Magic of Abramelin the Mage* (London: Watkins, 1898).
Wikimedia Commons
